one_line_takeaway: Splitting functions into 3-line-context code chunks and fine-tuning GraphCodeBERT on LLM-labeled data yields a vulnerability detector that localizes multiple flaws per function and reportedly beats CodeBERT/BERT/VUDENC/LineVul/VulBERTa, averaging ~89% accuracy across six datasets.
key_finding: Across all six datasets FuncVul ranks first on most metrics — best in 25 of 30 metric-dataset cases — with per-dataset F1 of 0.8888/0.8917/0.8657/0.8698/0.9013/0.9196 (datasets 1-6), giving a reported average accuracy of 89.39% and average F1 of 88.94%. The chunk-based approach dramatically beats whole-function modeling on the same data (Dataset 1: +53.9% accuracy, +42.0% F1; Dataset 2: +35.22% accuracy, +32.26% F1). Code chunks slightly beat generic chunks (~2% F1). The 3-line extended chunk (average length 6.2 lines) gives the best accuracy/precision/F1 among tested lengths (1,3,5,7,9,10,15,20,25). On unseen data FuncVul reaches 81.95% accuracy / 90.20% recall on new CVEs (Test Case 1) and 76.69% accuracy on new project IDs (Test Case 2).
core_contribution: FuncVul is a function-level software vulnerability detection model that operates on small 'code chunks' rather than whole functions. Its own novel contributions are: (1) a code-chunk representation ('3-line extended based code chunk': the edited line(s) plus 3 lines before/after when the edit is <=10 lines, else just the edited lines) that lets a single function be split into multiple chunks so multiple vulnerabilities per function can be flagged and localized; (2) a dual-strategy LLM-assisted data-labeling pipeline (using Gemini 1.5 Pro) that produces six datasets, either by intersecting patch-removed lines with LLM-predicted vulnerable lines, or by using the LLM alone; (3) a 'generic code chunk' variant that renames functions to F1..Fm and variables to v1..vn to standardize across naming conventions and languages; and (4) a detector built by fine-tuning the pre-trained GraphCodeBERT model on these chunks. Note: models like VulBERTa, VUDENC, LineVul, Devign, ReGVD, VulDeePecker, and GraphCodeBERT itself are cited prior work / baselines, not this paper's contribution — the paper's own novelty is the chunk-based framing plus the LLM-driven dataset construction, with GraphCodeBERT used off-the-shelf as the fine-tuning backbone.
threat_model: Software supply-chain attacks in which adversaries inject or leave vulnerable code inside widely used open-source packages/libraries (C/C++ and Python). The paper argues that identifying the specific vulnerable FUNCTION (and the specific vulnerable lines within it) — rather than just flagging a vulnerable package or an entire function — lets defenders prioritize and remediate faster. FuncVul is a defensive detection tool; it assumes access to function source code and, for dataset construction, to CVE patch/commit information from advisory databases (OSV.dev). A stated difficulty in the threat model: developers often bundle unrelated changes into fix commits, so patch diffs are noisy signals of the actual vulnerability.
limitations: Binary detection only — no multi-class severity/type classification (acknowledged as future work). Ground-truth labels are LLM-generated (Gemini 1.5 Pro), so label quality is bounded by the LLM and by the single-patch heuristic, which the authors admit 'does not guarantee' chunks are truly vulnerable. Restricting to single-commit, single-file CVE patches discards a large share of real-world multi-file/multi-commit vulnerabilities. Non-vulnerable samples partly come from random 5-10 line snippets, which may be an easy negative class inflating scores. Performance drops noticeably on cross-project generalization (76.69%). RQ6 (multiple vulnerabilities per function) is argued conceptually from the chunking design rather than backed by a quantitative multi-vuln benchmark. Only C/C++ and Python; small single-machine training setup. Reported MCC values for FuncVul (e.g. 0.9477, 0.9556, 0.9619) are anomalously higher than its accuracy/F1 and than baselines, suggesting possible inconsistency in the reported table.

datasets_used: Six self-constructed datasets curated from GitHub project source code and the OSV.dev vulnerability advisory database, labeled with Gemini 1.5 Pro
Dataset 1: code chunk, code+description prompt, LLM+patch — 1810 vulnerable (43.4%) / 2357 non-vulnerable (56.6%)
Dataset 2: code chunk, code-only prompt, LLM+patch — 2120 (42.6%) / 2851 (57.4%)
Dataset 3: generic code chunk, code+description, LLM+patch — 1810 (43.4%) / 2357 (56.6%)
Dataset 4: generic code chunk, code-only, LLM+patch — 2120 (42.6%) / 2851 (57.4%)
Dataset 5: code chunk, code+description, LLM-only labeling — 3169 (50%) / 3169 (50%)
Dataset 6: code chunk, code-only, LLM-only labeling — 6041 (50%) / 6041 (50%)
RQ4 held-out tests on Dataset 2: Test Case 1 (new CVEs) 1245 vulnerable / 1753 non-vulnerable; Test Case 2 (new project IDs) 179 / 280
quantitative_results: Reported average across six datasets: 89.39% accuracy, 88.94% F1 (abstract range 87-92% accuracy, 86-92% F1)
FuncVul ranked best in 25 of 30 metric-dataset cases
FuncVul per-dataset accuracy: DS1 0.8906, DS2 0.9022, DS3 0.8723, DS4 0.8797, DS5 0.9004, DS6 0.9184
Chunk vs full-function (Dataset 1): +53.9% accuracy, +42.8% precision, +35.5% recall, +42.0% F1
Chunk vs full-function (Dataset 2): +35.22% accuracy, +28.16% precision, +35.59% recall, +32.26% F1
Code chunk vs generic chunk (DS1 vs DS3): +2% accuracy, +1.84% precision, +2.96% recall, +1.9% F1
Cross-CVE generalization (Test Case 1): 0.8195 accuracy, 0.7283 precision, 0.9020 recall, 0.8059 F1 (419 FP, 122 FN)
Cross-project generalization (Test Case 2): 0.7669 accuracy, 0.6552 precision, 0.8492 recall, 0.7397 F1 (80 FP, 27 FN)
3-line extended chunk best overall; average chunk length 6.2 lines
80.04% (6515 of 8139) of OSV.dev CVEs have a single Git commit patch
baselines_compared: LineVul (Transformer line-level; best recall in four cases)
